title: Does the Concept of the Geofront actually make sense
display_title: Does the Concept of the Geofront actually make sense
wikitext:
[]: Does the Concept of the Geofront actually make sense?             Quote Post by Sicarius VI » Thu Nov 05, 2015 8:51 pm Just something I recently thought about, after reading Chuck's comment on another thread. Chuckman wrote: If you push it too far, the construction of the Geofront makes no sense. There's now way you could build something with that internal volume with no supports, even a dome, and where are the launch tubes for the Evangelions? If they exit all over the city they should stick up through the Geofront all over the place.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Nov 05, 2015 9:07 pm If it's the strongest material known to man and doesn't need supports, how did they dig through it? Where did they put all the dirt they dug out?

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Thu Nov 05, 2015 9:20 pm I thought the Geofront was already hollowed out the way it was when they found it? If not, it is still possible the Geofront was hollow, but filled with earth that was dug away at until we get to the point we see in the show.

[]:             Quote Post by Sicarius VI » Thu Nov 05, 2015 9:22 pm ^^Maybe they used it to reinforce the coast line... They need more of that after second impact. And you just help, if it was the strongest material, then HOW did they dig through it? Also considering how deep into the Earth/Black Moon, the pressure at the bottom at Terminal Dogma would also be in question, because wouldn't there be pressure all the way down there? Edit- Ninja'd @Sachi, I always assumed that part of it was open, and they had to dig out the rest of it. Even then, there is a considerable amount of construction that is in the "walls" of the Geofront, like the Eva tunnels, etc.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Nov 05, 2015 9:27 pm Sachi wrote: I thought the Geofront was already hollowed out the way it was when they found it? If not, it is still possible the Geofront was hollow, but filled with earth that was dug away at until we get to the point we see in the show.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Thu Nov 05, 2015 9:30 pm Alien geometries. Ponder not the obscene mathematics of the GeoFront, lest you go mad from the revelation.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Thu Nov 05, 2015 9:30 pm Sicarius VI wrote: @Sachi, I always assumed that part of it was open, and they had to dig out the rest of it. Even then, there is a considerable amount of construction that is in the "walls" of the Geofront, like the Eva tunnels, etc.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Thu Nov 05, 2015 9:45 pm I ran into that logical issue while writing fanfiction: it began to really bother me that I had no clue how the damn tunnels were supposed to work, so it eventually had to be written off with 'as the plot requires.' In all seriousness, though, I think 'alien geometries' may be a plausible argument, to a degree: we've seen the Angels manipulate and alter the laws of physics on multiple occasions (and the Evangelion themselves are violations, altering every rule in the book concerning the Square Cube Law, basic aerodynamics, etc.). It's acknowledged in-universe as being a sort of violation. Perhaps the interior of the GeoFront (being part of the Black Moon) doesn't occupy the same 'reality' as the rest of the world does, and might be a pocket dimension (or multiple pocket dimensions). Or it's outright affected by whatever is within the Black Moon itself. Heck, it could be a reason for why everyone is seemingly going crazy: if being constantly in the presence of the brain-dead body of the Mother God isn't enough, they work in a reality-warping pocket of no-space.

[]:             Quote Post by Sicarius VI » Thu Nov 05, 2015 10:10 pm Sachi wrote: IMO, the Eva tunnels don't need to be based in logic. It seems way ridiculous to me if they really run from Nerv HQ horizontal to the inner wall of the Geofront and then up along the Geofront wall to the city. I'm going to continue to pretend that they run straight up from Nerv HQ directly to the city with plot magic.

[]:             Quote Post by Iuvenal » Thu Nov 05, 2015 10:50 pm Sicarius VI wrote: How did they finish the construction [of the geofront] within 10 years[?]

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Nov 05, 2015 11:14 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: I ran into that logical issue while writing fanfiction: it began to really bother me that I had no clue how the damn tunnels were supposed to work, so it eventually had to be written off with 'as the plot requires.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Thu Nov 05, 2015 11:15 pm ^ That's a clever idea, actually: something that acts as an added structural support with the double-purpose of providing multiple deployment points. I like it.

[]:             Quote Post by Ray » Thu Nov 05, 2015 11:49 pm It's not supposed to make sense. It's mean to be a slightly more realistic version of the secret bases giant robot sentai superheroes operate out of.

[]:             Quote Post by Sicarius VI » Fri Nov 06, 2015 12:13 am Dirt tubes make sense, clearly thought wasn't put into the lore of Evangelion in general, let alone the Geofront to make sense. IMO, one hook of NGE when I first saw it was Shinji saying "OH BOY A GEOFRONT!..." in episode 1. Don't know why, but it kept me watching. Shame the way the Geofront is presented isn't possible, although I doubt any country or corporation would need a cave that big for anything.

[]:             Quote Post by unz » Fri Nov 06, 2015 11:14 am Ray wrote: It's not supposed to make sense. It's mean to be a slightly more realistic version of the secret bases giant robot sentai superheroes operate out of.

[]:             Quote Post by BlueBasilisk » Fri Nov 06, 2015 11:25 am Gob Hobblin wrote: I ran into that logical issue while writing fanfiction: it began to really bother me that I had no clue how the damn tunnels were supposed to work, so it eventually had to be written off with 'as the plot requires.'

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Nov 06, 2015 11:36 am This is the most relevant official sketch of the geofront but even there, we have some issues The diagram shows a sketch of the NERV geofront, a sphere notionally 89% full according to ep 20 i.e. 11% of it is open as a spherical cap. From the latter page we see that the volume of a spherical cap of height h (VM in the diagram) cut from a sphere radius R (OE or OV) is πh²(3R-h)/3; the volume of the whole sphere is 4πR³/3. If we let x = h/R, then volume of cap/volume of sphere is x²(3-x)/4, = 0.11 in our case. A little computation shows that x ~ 0.412, so OM = 0.588R, and since OME is a right-angled triangle, (ME/R)² = 1 - (0.588)², so ME is 0.809R. But it is also claimed that The Geofront is a sphere with a diameter of 13.75 km). However, 89% of it is buried and only the top part (900 m high and 6 km across) is open.

[]:             Quote Post by Lavinius » Fri Nov 06, 2015 11:50 am Chuckman wrote: I just added support columns that double as Eva launch tube... uh... dirt... tubes.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Nov 06, 2015 1:31 pm It's a big open space in the show, there aren't any columns holding up the roof.

[]:             Quote Post by Iuvenal » Fri Nov 06, 2015 2:05 pm Material-wise, what do we think the shell is made of? The empty part forms basically a dome, and while IANA M E I think that for a true dome you've basically got a compressive load on the top half and a tensive load on the bottom half (with the magnitude forming a gradient and an infinitessimal zone of zero stress at the border) with practically no shear load. A material with a super-high Young's modulus but a low hardness (good luck finding this material, which by the way also should by super-light), or even better a material where the Young's modulus isn't the same in all directions that is properly oriented to the direction of the stress applied by the dome, you could solve the problem of making the dome portion strong enough to avoid collapse while also being possible to dig (cut, really) through. Of course, with a radius of seven kilometers the required strength becomes astronomical and probably impractical on its face without even running the numbers. If you did generate this supermaterial, however, we need to make sure its bulk modulus is pretty high too, because the portion buried under nine miles of dirt and rock needs to hold up and not propagate cracks that will weaken the dome (look up fracture mechanics sometime when you're bored). Like every unsolved problem in materials science, the answer probably comes down to
